1.2: System validates that `email` exists in the `users` table via the Better Auth account adapter.
Password comparison uses Better Auth's configured hashing algorithm (scrypt by default; salted, constant-time compare).
Authentication failure (unknown email OR wrong password) returns HTTP 401 referencing `MSG-AUTH-01` without leaking which factor failed.
After `auth.api.signInEmail({ body: { email, password } })` succeeds, a session token is created and returned to the client.
1.7: Email uniqueness is enforced at the database layer via a `UNIQUE` constraint on `users.email`.
A pre-flight check inside `auth.api.signUpEmail({ body: { email, password, name } })` rejects sign-up when the email already exists.
Duplicate email returns HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-AUTH-02`; no account is created and no password hash is computed.
6.1: `address` is a required non-empty string (1â€“255 chars, trimmed); `unit` is optional (max 50 chars); `instructions` is optional (max 500 chars).
Class-validator decorators on `DeliveryAddressDto` run before the controller body executes.
6.2: `lat` and `lon` are required, finite numbers within `lat âˆˆ [-90, 90]` and `lon âˆˆ [-180, 180]`.
For SoLi's Vietnam-only operational scope, both coordinates must lie within Vietnam's bounding box (`lat âˆˆ [8.18, 23.39]`, `lon âˆˆ [102.14, 109.46]`).
Supplying one coordinate without the other, or any out-of-bounds value, returns HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ADDR-02`.
6.8: At checkout the chosen delivery address is **snapshot-copied** onto the `orders` row (`address`, `unit`, `instructions`, `lat`, `lon`).
Subsequent edits to the customer's address book DO NOT propagate to historical orders.
Deleting or mutating an address that is referenced by an active in-flight order is rejected with HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-ADDR-04`.
6.9: A customer may save up to 10 named addresses (e.g. *home*, *work*, custom labels); exceeding the limit returns HTTP 422 referencing `MSG-ADDR-05`.
The default-address flag is mutually exclusive â€” setting another address as default unflags the previous one in the same DB transaction.
Deletion is soft (sets `deletedAt`); orders that already snapshotted the address retain their copy (see BR-6.8).
Create, update, and delete operations persist immediately and return the updated address book referencing `MSG-ADDR-06`.
8.1: `idempotencyKey` is optional but, when present, must be a UUID v4; `couponCode` is optional; `notes` is optional (â‰¤ 500 chars).
Class-validator decorators on `CheckoutDto` validate every field before the handler runs; failures return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ORD-01`.
8.3: Idempotency key is derived as `IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_PREFIX + customerId + ':' + cartId` (or the explicit client-supplied key, when present).
`PlaceOrderHandler` calls `RedisService.setnx(key, orderId, IDEMPOTENCY_TTL_FALLBACK_SECONDS)` to atomically reserve the key.
If the key already exists, the previously persisted `orderId` is returned with HTTP 200 â€” the checkout pipeline is NOT executed twice (replay behaviour, see BR-8.4).
8.5: Cart lock key is `CART_KEY_PREFIX + customerId + CART_LOCK_SUFFIX`.
`PlaceOrderHandler` acquires the lock via `RedisService.setnx(lockKey, sessionId, CART_LOCK_TTL_SECONDS)` (Redis SET NX EX semantics).
Failure to acquire the lock indicates another checkout is in flight for the same customer; the handler returns HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-ORD-03`.
The lock is released after the checkout transaction commits or rolls back.
8.14: All prices, modifier deltas, shipping fees, and discount amounts are **recomputed server-side** from the Ordering ACL snapshot at activity (8). Client-supplied prices are ignored.
`itemsTotal = Î£ (item.unitPrice Ã— item.quantity + Î£ option.priceDelta)`; `totalAmount = itemsTotal + shippingFee âˆ’ discountAmount`.
`totalAmount` MUST be greater than 0; non-positive totals return HTTP 422 referencing `MSG-ORD-12`.
All VND amounts are stored and transmitted as positive integers (multiples of 1 000).
16.1: Required fields: full name (1â€“80 chars, trimmed), national ID number (12 digits), driver licence number, vehicle type, licence plate in canonical Vietnamese format (`XX-NNN.NN`).
Optional fields: bank account details for payouts; emergency contact.
Phone number must match the Vietnamese mobile pattern (e.g. `^(\+84|0)[3-9][0-9]{8}$`).
Class-validator decorators on `ShipperApplicationDto` enforce these constraints; failures return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-SHIP-01`.
16.2: The application must include four image references: national ID front, national ID back, driver licence, vehicle photo.
Each reference is a Cloudinary public ID validated through `ImageService` before the application row is inserted.
Missing or unresolvable references return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-SHIP-02`.
16.3: A user with an existing `shipper_applications` row in `status âˆˆ {pending_approval, approved}` cannot submit a new application.
The duplicate check runs inside the same DB transaction as the insertion (unique partial index on `(userId)` `WHERE status IN ('pending_approval', 'approved')`).
Duplicate submission returns HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-SHIP-08`. A previously `rejected` application allows resubmission.
16.6: The admin queue endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`; non-admins return HTTP 403 referencing `MSG-AUTH-08`.
Queue results are filtered by `status` (default `pending_approval`) with pagination (`limit âˆˆ [1, 100]`, `offset â‰¥ 0`).
Each row resolves applicant identity, vehicle type, licence plate, submission timestamp, and signed CDN URLs for documents.
16.8: An already-approved or already-rejected application returns HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-ADM-30` without mutating data.
Decision atomically updates the application row and (for approval) elevates the applicant's role inside a single DB transaction (see BR-16.9).
16.9: On approval, the applicant's primary `users.role` is updated to `shipper` (or `shipper` is appended to `users.soliRoles` for multi-role accounts).
Elevation occurs in the same DB transaction as the application state change; failure of either rolls back both.
After commit, `eventBus.publish(new ShipperApprovedEvent(shipperId))` is dispatched so downstream BCs (delivery dispatch, notifications) can react.
17.2: The endpoint validates that the caller holds an `approved` shipper application (UC-16) before any availability mutation.
Callers without role `shipper`, or with a non-approved application, return HTTP 403 referencing `MSG-SHIP-04`.
The guard runs before any availability state change is persisted.
17.4: Before allowing the `online â†’ offline` transition, the system queries orders owned by this shipper with `status âˆˆ {picked_up, delivering}` (composite index on `(shipperId, status)` for â‰¤ 100 ms response).
A non-empty result blocks the transition and surfaces `MSG-SHIP-07` (see BR-17.3).
An empty result allows the transition to proceed.
17.5: Once the in-flight check passes, the shipper's availability row is updated atomically to `offline`.
The shipper is removed from the dispatch candidate pool consumed by UC-18.
The transition publishes `ShipperAvailabilityChangedEvent` (see BR-17.8) so dependent services synchronise.
18.7: The order row is loaded with its current `version` value before any state mutation.
The transition UPDATE is `UPDATE orders SET status = 'picked_up', shipperId = $sid, version = version + 1 WHERE id = $oid AND version = $expected`.
Loss of the optimistic lock (zero rows updated) is treated as a race and falls through to the contention handler (BR-18.9).
21.10: Cancellation uses optimistic locking via `WHERE id = $oid AND version = $expected` on the `orders` UPDATE.
Concurrent cancellation attempts result in zero rows updated; the loser receives HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-CANC-04`.
The successful transition increments `version` and writes the `order_status_logs` audit row atomically within the same DB transaction.
After `CommandBus.execute(new TransitionOrderCommand(orderId, 'T-CANCEL', session, dto))` succeeds, the order's version is advanced; otherwise the handler retries or fails the request.
22.1: Optional fields: `comment` (â‰¤ 1 000 chars, trimmed); `tags` (array of pre-defined review tags, max 5 entries).
Class-validator decorators on `SubmitReviewDto` enforce these constraints; failures return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-RATE-01`.
22.8: A single `UNIQUE` constraint on `reviews(orderId)` enforces one review per order at the database layer.
A duplicate INSERT raises a constraint violation that the application layer maps to HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-RATE-03`.
This is the authoritative duplicate guard; the application-side optimistic check in BR-22.9 is best-effort only.
22.11: The review INSERT and the `restaurants.averageRating` / `reviewCount` projection UPDATE run inside the same DB transaction.
After commit, `eventBus.publish(new ReviewSubmittedEvent(reviewId, restaurantId, customerId, stars))` is dispatched.
The Notification BC subscribes to deliver a `new_review` notification to the restaurant owner.
22.12: A read-side projection maintains `restaurants.averageRating` (decimal) and `restaurants.reviewCount` (integer) columns.
On each new review the projector applies the running update `avg' = (avg Ã— count + stars) / (count + 1)` and `count' = count + 1` in the same transaction as the review INSERT.
UC-2 (Discover Restaurants) reads from these denormalised columns to avoid a join on every list query.
22.13: The `reviews` schema includes `moderationStatus âˆˆ {visible, hidden, flagged}` (default `visible`) and `moderationReason` (nullable text).
Reviews flagged by automated checks (profanity, length, duplicate content) move to `flagged` and require admin moderation before becoming publicly visible again.
`hidden` reviews are excluded from the rating projection (BR-22.12) and from public listing.
24.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
Role is verified via `hasRole(session.user.role, 'admin')` on every request variant (list, create, update, lifecycle).
25.11: The `completed â†’ refund_pending` transition uses `UPDATE payment_transactions SET status = 'refund_pending', version = version + 1, refundInitiatedAt = now() WHERE id = $id AND version = $expected AND status = 'completed'`.
Lock loss (zero rows updated) means another concurrent handler won the race; the loser exits silently (idempotent â€” see BR-25.13).
After `PaymentTransactionRepository.create(data)` (or the version-guarded UPDATE) succeeds, the gateway call proceeds.
27.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
After `auth.api.getSession({ headers })` extracts the session, role is verified via `hasRole(session.user.role, 'admin')`.
27.11: Each approval/unapproval appends an audit row carrying `adminId`, `restaurantId`, `previousState`, `newState`, `decidedAt`, and (optionally) `reasonNote`.
The audit row is inserted in the same DB transaction as the approval UPDATE; failure of either rolls back both.
Audit data supports compliance review and admin performance reporting.
28.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
The role check is re-verified on each approve/reject request to prevent privilege drift between requests.
28.3: The queue returns `shipper_applications` rows with `status = 'pending_approval'` by default (filterable to `approved` / `rejected`).
Each row includes applicant identity, vehicle type, licence plate, signed CDN URLs for documents, and submission timestamp.
Pagination defaults to 20 rows (max 100); the list response references `MSG-ADM-05`.
28.5: The detail endpoint returns the full application payload including national-ID images, driver licence, vehicle photo (resolved via `ImageService` to signed CDN URLs valid for 1 hour), and applicant contact data.
Signed URLs are scoped to admin role and cannot be reused outside the request's session context.
28.8: Rejection requires a non-empty `reason` (1â€“500 chars, trimmed).
Missing or whitespace-only reason returns HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-SHIP-01`.
The reason is persisted in `shipper_applications.rejectionReason` for audit (see BR-28.10).
28.11: Approval atomically updates `shipper_applications.status = 'approved'` AND elevates the applicant's role on `users` (primary `role` and/or `soliRoles`) inside a single DB transaction.
Failure of either update rolls back both; `ShipperApprovedEvent` is published only after the transaction commits.
Idempotent: re-approving an already-approved row is a no-op and does not republish the event.
28.15: Each decision appends an audit row to `shipper_approval_audit` carrying `adminId`, `applicationId`, `userId`, `decision` (`approved` / `rejected`), `reason`, `decidedAt`.
The audit row is inserted in the same DB transaction as the application state change.
Audit data supports admin performance review and applicant appeal workflows.
29.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
The role check is re-verified on each suspend / reactivate request.
29.2: The target user must exist in the `users` table.
A non-existent `userId` returns HTTP 404 referencing `MSG-ADM-13` (see BR-29.16).
The handler loads the user record before evaluating any further rule (validation, safeguard, persistence).
29.3: `reason` is required (1â€“500 chars, trimmed); missing or whitespace-only returns HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ADM-12`.
`expiresAt` is optional; when provided it MUST be a valid future timestamp â€” past dates return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ADM-12`.
`expiresAt = null` means permanent suspension; a future value means temporary suspension (auto-expiry via BR-29.15).
29.5: Suspension persists `users.banned = true`, `banReason`, `banExpires`, `bannedAt`, and `bannedBy` atomically inside one DB transaction.
The transaction also terminates active sessions (BR-29.6) and inserts the audit row (BR-29.14).
After commit, `eventBus.publish(new PartnerSuspendedEvent(partnerId, partnerType))` is dispatched (see BR-29.7).
29.6: On suspension, every active session belonging to `userId` is deleted from the Better Auth session store inside the same DB transaction as the user-row update.
Subsequent requests bearing those tokens receive HTTP 401 referencing `MSG-AUTH-05`.
The customer / restaurant / shipper apps must redirect to the sign-in screen on the next 401.
29.9: Reactivation is allowed only when `users.banned = true`; reactivating an already-active user returns HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-ADM-31`.
Reactivation is admin-only; the suspended user cannot self-reactivate.
A non-empty `reason` note is required on the reactivation endpoint to support audit (see BR-29.14).
29.14: Each suspend / reactivate decision appends a row to `user_status_audit` carrying `adminId`, `userId`, `action` (`suspended` / `reactivated`), `reason`, `expiresAt`, `decidedAt`.
The audit row is inserted in the same DB transaction as the user-row mutation.
Audit data supports compliance review, dispute resolution, and admin performance reporting.
29.15: A scheduled task `PartnerSuspensionTask` runs once per minute, scanning users where `banned = true AND banExpires <= now()`.
For each match, the task clears `banned`, `banReason`, `banExpires` atomically and dispatches `PartnerReactivatedEvent`.
The task records the count of automatic reactivations per run; failures are logged but never propagate.
30.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
After `auth.api.getSession({ headers })` extracts the session, role is verified via `hasRole(session.user.role, 'admin')`.
31.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
The role check is re-verified on every endpoint variant (list, detail, profile edit) â€” see BR-31.20.
31.3: Filter fields: `query` (free-text on `email` and `name`), `role` (single canonical value), `status âˆˆ {active, suspended, pending}`, `createdFrom`, `createdTo`, `limit âˆˆ [1, 100]`, `offset â‰¥ 0`.
All filters compose conjunctively (`AND`); empty filter values are ignored.
Free-text `query` performs case-insensitive prefix matching on `email` and `name`.
31.4: Invalid filter values return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ADM-18` and never reach the database.
Date range violations (`createdFrom > createdTo`) return HTTP 400 with the same message.
31.7: The detail endpoint requires a valid `userId` in UUID format.
A non-existent `userId` returns HTTP 404 referencing `MSG-ADM-13` (see BR-31.8).
31.11: The detail view presents action buttons that delegate to other UCs: **Suspend / Reactivate** â†’ UC-29; **Change Role** â†’ UC-35; **View Orders** â†’ UC-30; **Refund Order** â†’ UC-32.
Each delegated action invokes its own endpoint and writes its own audit row; UC-31 does NOT proxy these calls.
UC-31 itself only handles list / detail / profile-edit operations (see BR-31.17).
31.13: `name` must be a non-empty trimmed string of 1â€“80 chars.
`contactEmail`, when provided, must be valid RFC 5322 AND globally unique (excluding the current user); duplicate returns HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-ADM-30`.
31.14: Profile changes persist atomically on `users` inside a single DB transaction; the transaction also writes the audit row (BR-31.18) and increments `users.version`.
After commit, `eventBus.publish(new UserProfileUpdatedEvent(userId, changedFields))` is dispatched.
The Notification BC consumes this event to refresh cached user-display data.
31.17: UC-31 endpoints do NOT handle role assignment (UC-35), suspension (UC-29), nor order operations (UC-30 / UC-32).
Any client attempting one of these via UC-31 receives HTTP 405 referencing `MSG-ADM-29`.
The admin client must navigate to the dedicated UC endpoint and present the appropriate confirmation modal.
31.18: Each profile edit appends a row to `user_profile_audit` carrying `adminId`, `userId`, `fieldChanges` (JSON of `{field: {from, to}}`), `editedAt`.
The audit row is inserted in the same DB transaction as the profile UPDATE.
Audit data supports compliance review and user-dispute resolution.
32.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
The role check is re-verified on each cancel and refund request.
33.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
The role check is re-verified on every report-view and export request.
33.2: `type âˆˆ {orders, financial, users, promotions}`; unsupported values return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ADM-24`.
`dateFrom` and `dateTo` are required ISO dates with `dateFrom â‰¤ dateTo`; the span must not exceed 366 days.
For export requests, `format âˆˆ {csv, xlsx}`; other values return HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ADM-22`.
33.5: **Orders Report** aggregates from `orders` and `order_status_logs`: daily order volume; completion rate (`delivered / total`); cancellation rate; average preparation time (`confirmed â†’ ready_for_pickup`); average delivery time (`picked_up â†’ delivered`); status breakdown.
Metrics are scoped to the supplied date range and optional filters (`restaurantId`, `paymentMethod`).
All time-bucket math uses the platform timezone (`Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh`).
33.6: **Financial Report** aggregates from `payment_transactions` and `orders`: daily GMV (gross merchandise value); platform commission per BR-5 (configurable %); payment-method breakdown (COD vs VNPay); refund volume.
Restaurant-scoped view shows GMV per restaurant ranked descending.
A reconciliation column flags discrepancies between `orders.totalAmount` and `payment_transactions.amount` for the same `orderId`.
33.7: **Users Report** â€” new registrations per role per day; pending approval counts (UC-27 restaurants + UC-28 shippers); active vs suspended account counts.
**Promotions Report** â€” active promotions; coupon-redemption rate (`coupon_codes` with `currentUses > 0`); discount value distributed (sum of `promotion_usages.discountAmount`).
Both reports honour the same date range and filter contract as the orders / financial reports.
33.8: Export `format âˆˆ {csv, xlsx}`; CSV uses UTF-8 with BOM and RFC 4180 escaping; XLSX uses OpenXML.
The export file contains the same rows the on-screen view rendered for the same parameters.
Each export row preserves the report's structural integrity (header row + data rows) so downstream tooling can ingest it directly.
33.12: Report data freshness is `â‰¤ 60 seconds` for KPIs derived from operational tables.
A short-lived per-`(type, params)` cache with TTL of 30 seconds prevents request stampedes from dashboard polling.
The cache key combines `type`, `dateFrom`, `dateTo`, and the canonicalised filter map; cache is invalidated on schema migrations.
34.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
The role check is re-verified on every dashboard request.
34.2: **Today** â€” order count, GMV, new registrations.
**Last 7 days** â€” order count, GMV, average order value, top 5 restaurants by revenue.
34.3: Pending restaurant approvals count = `restaurants WHERE isApproved = false`.
Pending shipper approvals count = `shipper_applications WHERE status = 'pending_approval'`.
New shippers approved in the last 7 days and new restaurants approved in the last 7 days (rolling-window counters).
34.4: Last 20 platform-wide events: orders placed, orders delivered, shippers approved, partners suspended.
Each entry shows actor, action, timestamp, and an entity reference (clickable link to the owning detail UC).
The stream is refreshed per request; no real-time push.
34.5: Successful retrieval returns the structured dashboard payload referencing `MSG-ADM-25`.
KPI values include both the current value and the previous-period delta for trend context.
34.7: KPI computation results are cached per `(admin, time-window)` for 30 seconds to absorb dashboard refresh polling.
The `live` counter section bypasses the cache to keep in-flight order counts accurate.
Cache entries for the `today` / `live` tiles are invalidated on every `OrderPlacedEvent` or `OrderStatusChangedEvent`.
35.1: Endpoint requires an authenticated session whose primary role is `admin`.
The role check is re-verified on every role-assignment call.
35.2: Optional: `soliRoles` (array, subset of the canonical role set).
`role = 'shipper'` is rejected â€” shipper elevation is reachable only via UC-28; attempting it here returns HTTP 400 referencing `MSG-ADM-28`.
35.4: Demoting the last active (`banned = false`) administrator account is rejected with HTTP 409 referencing `MSG-ADM-27`.
The check is executed inside the same DB transaction that performs the role change so a concurrent demotion cannot create a platform lockout (no TOCTOU race).
The action is allowed when at least one other active admin remains after the change.
35.6: Target user must exist; a non-existent `userId` returns HTTP 404 referencing `MSG-ADM-13`.
Admin loads the user record before evaluating safeguards (BR-35.4), suspension state (BR-35.5), or persistence (BR-35.7).
35.7: Primary `users.role` and operational `users.soliRoles` are updated in a single DB transaction that also writes the audit row (BR-35.10) and increments `users.version`.
After commit, `eventBus.publish(new RoleChangedEvent(userId, role, action))` is dispatched (see BR-35.8).
Failure of any step rolls back the entire transaction.
35.9: After a successful role change, all active sessions belonging to `userId` are flagged for refresh.
The next request bearing an existing session token triggers a re-fetch of role data from `users` so authorisation decisions reflect the new role set immediately.
Sessions are NOT invalidated (no forced logout); only their cached role data refreshes.
35.10: Each role change appends a row to `role_assignment_audit` carrying `adminId`, `userId`, `previousRole`, `previousSoliRoles`, `newRole`, `newSoliRoles`, `reason`, `decidedAt`.
The audit row is inserted in the same DB transaction as the role UPDATE.
Audit data supports compliance review and user dispute resolution.
